title: What Is PD in Glasses? Pupillary Distance Explained Simply
description: PD (pupillary distance) is the distance between your pupils in millimeters. Learn why it matters, how to measure it, and what happens if it's wrong.
tldr: PD (pupillary distance) is the distance between the centres of your pupils, measured in millimeters. Adults typically have a PD between 54-74mm. Your optician uses this measurement to centre your lenses precisely in front of your pupils. Even a 2mm error can cause eye strain, headaches, and blurry peripheral vision — especially with progressive or high-prescription lenses.

tldrStats.label: Only needs measuring once (rarely changes in adults)

definition.text: The measurement in millimeters between the centres of the left and right pupils. Used by opticians to align the optical centres of prescription lenses with the wearer's line of sight. Can be measured as a single number (binocular PD) or as two separate values (monocular PD: right and left individually).

sections.content:
Pupillary distance — PD for short — is simply the distance between the centres of your two pupils. It's measured in millimeters, and for most adults it falls between 54mm and 74mm.

Your optician needs this number to position your lenses correctly in the frame. The optical centre of each lens (the point of clearest, most accurate vision) must align with your pupil. If the centres are off, you're looking through a part of the lens that wasn't designed for straight-ahead viewing.

PD comes in two forms:

  • Binocular PD: A single number measuring the total distance between both pupils (e.g., 63mm). This is the most commonly used format.
  • Monocular PD: Two separate numbers — one for each eye's distance from the centre of the nose bridge (e.g., 31mm/32mm). More precise because most faces aren't perfectly symmetrical. Critical for progressive lenses.

If you've had an eye exam, your optician measured your PD during the fitting process. It's part of the complete prescription information, even though some prescriptions don't explicitly list it.

sections.h2: Why PD Matters for Your Glasses
sections.content:
Lenses work by bending light to a focal point on your retina. The optical centre of the lens is the point where light passes through with the least distortion. When this centre aligns with your pupil, you see clearly. When it doesn't, several problems occur:

  • Eye strain: Your eye muscles work harder to compensate for the misaligned optical centre. This leads to fatigue, especially during extended reading or screen use.
  • Headaches: The extra effort from your eye muscles can trigger tension headaches, particularly with new glasses.
  • Blurry peripheral vision: Looking through off-centre lens areas introduces prismatic effect — a slight bending of the image that worsens toward the lens edges.
  • Dizziness or nausea: With strong prescriptions, significant PD errors can cause a disorienting prismatic imbalance between the two eyes.

The higher your prescription, the more PD accuracy matters. Someone with a -1.00 prescription might not notice a 2mm PD error. Someone with a -6.00 prescription will definitely feel it. And for progressive lenses, PD precision is non-negotiable — the corridor of intermediate and near vision is narrow, and even small misalignment makes the lens unusable.

Your optician measures PD using a pupillometer (a device you look into) or a ruler-based method. This is the most accurate approach and is included in any professional fitting.

If you need to measure it yourself — for online ordering, for example — here's the basic method:

  1. Stand 20cm (8 inches) from a mirror
  2. Hold a ruler flat against your brow, just above your eyes
  3. Close your right eye. Align the ruler's 0mm mark with the centre of your left pupil
  4. Without moving the ruler, close your left eye and open your right
  5. Read the number at the centre of your right pupil — that's your binocular PD

Repeat three times and average the results for better accuracy. For monocular PD, measure from the centre of each pupil to the centre of your nose bridge separately.

Important caveat: Self-measurement is approximate. It's fine for simple single vision prescriptions but risky for progressives, bifocals, or prescriptions above +/-4.00. For these, professional measurement at an optical store is strongly recommended.

Sometimes, but not always. In Canada, optometrists are required to give you your prescription upon request, but PD is technically a fitting measurement, not a prescription value. Some offices include it; others don't.

If your prescription doesn't list PD, you have options:

  • Ask your optometrist's office. They likely measured it and can provide it — though some may charge a small fee since it's considered a dispensing measurement rather than part of the exam.
  • Have it measured at any optical store. Quick and usually free if you're considering buying frames there.
  • Measure it yourself. Acceptable for simple prescriptions (see method above).

When you buy glasses from a store like Charm Optical, the optician measures your PD as part of the fitting — you don't need to worry about it being on your written prescription. The measurement is taken with your chosen frame on your face, which gives the most accurate result.

Not all lenses are equally sensitive to PD accuracy:

  • Single vision (low Rx): Most forgiving. A 1-2mm error is barely noticeable for prescriptions under +/-3.00.
  • Single vision (high Rx): More sensitive. Prescriptions above +/-4.00 need accurate PD to avoid noticeable prismatic effects.
  • Progressive lenses: PD must be measured monocularly (each eye separately) and vertically (segment height). The progressive corridor is only about 12-14mm wide — even small misalignment means you can't find the sweet spots for intermediate and reading vision.
  • Bifocal lenses: Somewhat forgiving for distance, but the reading segment position depends on accurate PD and segment height.

This is one reason why in-store professional fitting matters most for progressive lenses. The optician marks the lens position while you're wearing the frame and looking straight ahead, capturing both horizontal PD and vertical position in one measurement.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

Your glasses are only as good as your prescription. An outdated or inaccurate prescription means blurry vision no matter how expensive your lenses are. The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults and annually for children, seniors, and anyone with diabetes or a family history of eye disease.

A comprehensive eye exam does far more than measure your prescription. Your optometrist checks the health of your retina, optic nerve, and blood vessels — catching conditions like glaucoma, macular degeneration, and diabetic retinopathy early, often before you notice any symptoms. They also measure your intraocular pressure, test your peripheral vision, and assess how your eyes work together as a team.

In Alberta, a comprehensive eye exam typically costs $100-$180 if not covered by insurance. Most employer plans cover the exam fee entirely. Children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers comprehensive eye exam at Charm Optical with experienced optometrists and direct-bills most direct insurance billing. book an eye exam online online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

The best next step is always a professional eye exam. Your optometrist can evaluate your specific needs and recommend the right combination of lenses, coatings, and frames for your lifestyle and budget. In Alberta, most employer insurance plans cover comprehensive eye exams, and children under 18 and adults over 65 receive coverage through Alberta Health.

Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists, a selection of over 500 designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, and Versace, and same-day glasses for many prescription types. They offer direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers, so you only pay the difference at the counter.

Whether you are buying your first pair of glasses, upgrading to progressive lenses, or just need an adjustment on your current frames, having a trusted local optician makes the process smoother. Book an appointment online — most appointments are available within a few days, and walk-ins are welcome for adjustments and frame browsing.

faqs.q: What is a normal PD for adults?
faqs.a: Adult PD typically ranges from 54-74mm, with the average being about 63mm. Women tend to have slightly smaller PDs (54-65mm) and men slightly larger (59-74mm). It stabilizes in late teens and rarely changes after that.
PD stabilizes by the late teens and doesn't meaningfully change in adults. Children's PD increases as they grow, which is why paediatric PD needs remeasuring with each new pair of glasses.

faqs.q: Can wrong PD damage your eyes?
faqs.a: Wrong PD won't permanently damage your eyes, but it can cause significant discomfort: eye strain, headaches, blurry peripheral vision, and difficulty adjusting to new glasses. Your eyes try to compensate for the misalignment, leading to fatigue.

faqs.q: How do I find my PD without going to an optician?
faqs.a: Stand in front of a mirror, hold a ruler against your brow, close one eye at a time, and measure from pupil centre to pupil centre. Repeat three times and average. This is approximate — for progressive lenses or strong prescriptions, professional measurement is much more reliable.

faqs.q: How often should I get my eyes checked?
faqs.a: Adults should get a comprehensive eye exam every 1-2 years. Children, seniors, diabetics, and those with family history of eye disease should go annually. Charm Optical in Edmonton offers thorough eye exams with experienced optometrists — book online at see.charmoptical.ca.

faqs.q: Can I use my prescription to buy glasses anywhere?
faqs.a: Yes, in Canada your prescription belongs to you and you can use it at any optical store. However, progressive lenses require additional measurements (PD, segment height) that must be taken in person. Charm Optical takes these measurements for free during your frame selection.
